---
title: "Detector Parameter Selection"
---

## Overview

WARA uses CUSUM-based detectors on each wavelet packet to identify attack-affected signal components. The detectors are configured with two parameters: a **mean shift** ($b_j$) that suppresses normal measurement fluctuations, and a **detection threshold** ($\tau$) that triggers an alarm when the cumulative statistic exceeds it.

Selecting appropriate parameters is critical: too sensitive and normal flight maneuvers trigger false alarms; too conservative and real attacks go undetected. WARA selects parameters based on **time to detection** (TTD) and **false positive rate** during attack-free flights.

## Parameter Selection Procedure

### Data Collection

We use a specially designed **maneuver mission** to gather attack-free flight data. This mission spans typical flight scenarios — takeoff, hover, cruise, and turning — and includes several successive sharp turns to stress-test flight performance. The diversity of conditions and emphasis on drastic maneuvers make this mission ideal for parameter selection, as detector behavior in attack-free flight is primarily affected by measurement noise and environmental disturbances.

We collect **10 attack-free flight records** in both SITL and HITL simulations, with fault isolation and recovery functionality disabled to ensure mission completion. For each wavelet packet, we extract the wavelet coefficients and residuals ($\bar{r}_j(t)$ and $r^{\prime}_j(t)$) to construct a validation dataset.

### Determining Candidate Mean Shifts

From the validation dataset, we derive candidate mean shifts for each wavelet packet:

- **LF packets (Trend):** Average amplitude of $\bar{r}_j(t)$ → candidate $\bar{b}_{P,j}$
- **LF packets (Transient):** Average amplitude of $r^{\prime}_j(t)$ → candidate $b^{\prime}_{P,j}$
- **HF packets:** Average amplitude of $|w_{P,j}(t)|$ for each HF packet; the smallest average is chosen as $b_{P,j}$ (representing the background noise level)

The cutoff frequency for trend features is set to $f_{CUTOFF} = 10$ Hz, consistent with the motion signal band.

### Threshold Selection (4 Steps)

1. **Find minimum thresholds** — For each candidate mean shift, use the validation dataset from each flight to identify the *minimum threshold* $\tau_{min}$ that avoids false alarms in that flight.

2. **Determine reference threshold** — Choose the *largest* $\tau_{min}$ across all 10 flights as the reference threshold $\tau_{ref}$. A detector configured with $\tau_{ref}$ guarantees zero false positives across all 10 missions.

3. **Evaluate and confirm** — Configure detectors with different candidate mean shifts and their corresponding $\tau_{ref}$ values. Evaluate TTD at various attack deviations, where $TTD = t_{alarm} - t_{atk}$ (the elapsed time from attack onset to first alarm). Select the mean shift that provides the best trade-off between detection speed and false positive avoidance.

4. **Apply safety margin** — Add a 5% safety margin to the selected $\tau_{ref}$:
   $$\tau = 1.05 \cdot \tau_{ref}$$

## Simulation Detector Parameters

Following the procedure above, the table below presents the detector parameters used in SITL and HITL experiments. A detection threshold of $\tau = 3.0$ is used uniformly across all wavelet packets.

<div style="overflow-x:auto">

<table style="min-width:700px;border-collapse:collapse;text-align:center">
<tr>
  <th rowspan="2" style="vertical-align:middle">Sensor</th>
  <th colspan="6">Multi-IMU (SITL)</th>
  <th colspan="4">Single-IMU (HITL)</th>
</tr>
<tr>
  <th colspan="2">LF (Trend)</th>
  <th colspan="2">LF (Transient)</th>
  <th colspan="2">HF (Lvl 4)</th>
  <th colspan="2">HF (Lvl 6)</th>
  <th colspan="2">HF (Lvl 4)</th>
</tr>
<tr>
  <th></th>
  <th>τ</th><th>bⱼ</th>
  <th>τ</th><th>bⱼ</th>
  <th>τ</th><th>bⱼ</th>
  <th>τ</th><th>bⱼ</th>
  <th>τ</th><th>bⱼ</th>
</tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"><b>Accel</b></td>
  <td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td>
</tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"><b>Gyro</b></td>
  <td>3.0</td><td>0.10</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.30</td><td>3.0</td><td>0.05</td>
</tr>
</table>

</div>

Key observations:
- Gyro LF (Trend) uses a higher mean shift ($b_j = 0.10$) than Accel, reflecting the gyroscope's greater sensitivity to drone maneuvers.
- Gyro HF (Lvl 6) in single-IMU mode requires a larger mean shift ($b_j = 0.30$) due to the absence of a redundant IMU for low-frequency signal cancellation — the detector must tolerate more benign variation.

## Real-World Gyroscope Detector Parameters

For the real-world quadcopters (F330 and ZD550), we apply the same procedure using 10 attack-free flight logs per drone. The table below lists the deployed gyroscope detector parameters, broken down by axis (X, Y, Z).

<div style="overflow-x:auto">

<table style="min-width:700px;border-collapse:collapse;text-align:center">
<tr>
  <th rowspan="2" style="vertical-align:middle">Drone</th>
  <th rowspan="2" style="vertical-align:middle">Param</th>
  <th colspan="3">LF (Trend)</th>
  <th colspan="3">LF (Transient)</th>
  <th colspan="3">HF (Lvl 4)</th>
</tr>
<tr>
  <th>X</th><th>Y</th><th>Z</th>
  <th>X</th><th>Y</th><th>Z</th>
  <th>X</th><th>Y</th><th>Z</th>
</tr>
<tr><td rowspan="2" style="vertical-align:middle;text-align:left"><b>F330</b></td>
  <td>τ</td>
  <td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
  <td>bⱼ</td>
  <td>0.20</td><td>0.14</td><td>0.06</td><td>0.05</td><td>0.05</td><td>0.03</td><td>0.40</td><td>0.30</td><td>0.10</td>
</tr>
<tr><td rowspan="2" style="vertical-align:middle;text-align:left"><b>ZD550</b></td>
  <td>τ</td>
  <td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td><td>3.0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
  <td>bⱼ</td>
  <td>0.15</td><td>0.10</td><td>0.06</td><td>0.05</td><td>0.03</td><td>0.04</td><td>0.25</td><td>0.15</td><td>0.15</td>
</tr>
</table>

</div>

The ZD550 uses slightly smaller mean shifts than the F330 across most axes and bands, reflecting the larger drone's more stable flight dynamics and lower vibration levels.
